title: Marcela Gracia and Elisa Sánchez marry in A Coruña
description: On 8 June 1901 Marcela Gracia Ibeas and Elisa Sánchez Loriga were wed in A Coruña, Galicia. To make the ceremony possible, Sánchez assumed the male identity of Mario Sánchez, the name entered on the marriage certificate.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: New York police raid the Ariston Hotel Baths
description: On 21 February 1903 police raided the Ariston Hotel Baths in New York City, the first recorded raid on a gay bathhouse in the United States. Thirty-four men were arrested, twelve stood trial on sodomy charges, and seven drew prison terms of four to twenty years.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Detained youths at Les Douaires riot against a same-sex relationship ban
description: In July 1905 young people held at the Les Douaires detention site in France rioted after the penal administration tried to ban homosexual relationships among detainees, an episode counted among the earliest recorded queer riots.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Imre, an early gay novel with a happy ending, is published
description: The novel Imre appeared in 1906 and is regarded as possibly the first openly gay American novel to close on a happy note for its protagonists.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Karl M. Baer undergoes pioneering gender-affirming surgery
description: In December 1906 Karl M. Baer became the first known transgender person to receive sex reassignment surgery.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Adolf Brand jailed after outing Chancellor von Bülow
description: Activist Adolf Brand of the Gemeinschaft der Eigenen, campaigning against Paragraph 175, published a piece in 1907 outing German chancellor Bernhard von Bülow. Von Bülow won a libel suit that cleared his name, while Brand served 18 months in prison.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Harden–Eulenburg affair unfolds in Germany
description: Between 1907 and 1909 a chain of libel trials, courts-martial and press campaigns over alleged homosexuality within Kaiser Wilhelm II's inner circle became known as the Harden–Eulenburg affair, feeding a major public scandal around homosexuality in Germany.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Emma Goldman speaks publicly for homosexual rights
description: From 1910 the anarchist Emma Goldman defended homosexual love in her public lectures in the United States. Magnus Hirschfeld later credited her as the first woman, indeed the first American, to take up that defense before a general audience.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Mormon journal prints a tribute to Sappho of Lesbos
description: In 1912 the Young Woman's Journal paid tribute to Sappho of Lesbos, the first explicit reference to lesbianism in a Mormon magazine.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jacob Schorer launches the Dutch Scientific Humanitarian Committee
description: Dr. Jacob Schorer set up the NWHK in 1912, the first organization in the Netherlands to campaign against discrimination toward homosexuals.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Alexander Berkman publishes Prison Memoirs of an Anarchist
description: Berkman's 1912 memoir treated love between men in prison with unusual openness; historian Terence Kissack ranks it among the most significant American political texts on homosexuality written before the 1950s.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Earliest known print use of the word faggot for gay men
description: A 1913 vocabulary of criminal slang published in Portland, Oregon contains the first documented use of the word in print in reference to gay men.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: October Revolution voids Russia's sodomy statute
description: The October Revolution of 1917 annulled the old imperial criminal code in its entirety, taking Article 995 against male homosexual acts with it. Bolshevik leaders of the period stated that homosexual and heterosexual relations stood equal before the law.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Magnus Hirschfeld opens the Institute for Sexual Science in Berlin
description: In 1919 Magnus Hirschfeld co-founded the Institut für Sexualwissenschaft in Berlin, a pioneering private research institute with a counseling office devoted to sexuality. Its library of thousands of volumes stood until Nazis destroyed it in May 1933.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gay film Different from the Others premieres
description: The 1919 German picture Different from the Others, among the first films to portray homosexuality openly, reached cinemas with Magnus Hirschfeld appearing in a cameo and partially financing the production.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: British attempt to criminalize lesbianism fails
description: A 1921 parliamentary move to make sexual acts between women illegal for the first time in Britain's history did not pass.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Soviet criminal code confirms decriminalization of homosexual acts
description: A new criminal code that came into force in the USSR in 1922 contained no offense covering consensual homosexual acts, formally decriminalizing them.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Earliest known print use of the word fag for gay men
description: Nels Anderson's 1923 study The Hobo contains the first documented printed use of the term in reference to gay men.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Elsa Gidlow publishes the first openly lesbian US poetry volume
description: English-born poet Elsa Gidlow brought out On a Grey Thread in 1923, the first volume of openly lesbian love poetry published in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Henry Gerber creates the Society for Human Rights in Chicago
description: In 1924 Henry Gerber established the Society for Human Rights in Chicago, the first homosexual rights organization in the United States. Police pressure forced the group to disband after only a few months.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Paraguay removes criminal penalties for homosexuality
description: Consensual homosexual acts ceased to be a criminal offense in Paraguay, an event this timeline dates to 1924; other references give differing years, so the date merits verification.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Peru removes criminal penalties for homosexuality
description: Peru's penal code of 1924 left consensual same-sex acts between adults outside the criminal law, legalizing homosexuality nationwide.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The New York Times prints the word homosexuality
description: In 1926 The New York Times became the first major publication to use the word homosexuality in its pages.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Police raid closes lesbian café Eve's Hangout
description: Eve's Hangout, a well-known lesbian café in Greenwich Village, shut its doors in 1926 following a police raid.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Karol Szymanowski appointed head of Poland's national music school
description: In 1927 Karol Szymanowski, Poland's openly gay leading composer, took charge of the state-owned Fryderyk Chopin Music Academy.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Radclyffe Hall's The Well of Loneliness is published
description: Radclyffe Hall's lesbian novel The Well of Loneliness came out in the UK in 1928 and later in the United States, provoking major legal controversy and moving homosexuality into public conversation.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Katharine Lee Bates, author of America the Beautiful, dies
description: The American poet Katharine Lee Bates, remembered for writing America the Beautiful, died on 22 May 1929.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Reichstag committee votes to strike Paragraph 175
description: On 16 October 1929 a Reichstag committee approved repealing Paragraph 175, the German statute penalizing male homosexuality. The vote was never implemented, as the Nazi rise to power intervened.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Las Carolinas stage the first documented LGBT demonstration
description: In 1931 a group of Barcelona transvestites known as Las Carolinas protested publicly after the demolition of a central public bath that had served as an LGBT meeting place — regarded as the first documented LGBT demonstration in history.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Pro-lesbian film Mädchen in Uniform is released
description: The 1931 German picture Mädchen in Uniform became one of the first explicitly lesbian films and the first to present lesbian love sympathetically.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Dora Richter receives the first known vaginoplasty
description: In Berlin in 1931 Dora Richter became the first transgender woman known to undergo vaginoplasty.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Poland codifies an equal age of consent
description: Poland's 1932 penal code set the age of consent at 15 for homosexual and heterosexual acts alike. Polish law itself had never criminalized homosexuality; occupying powers had outlawed it in 1835.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Denmark decriminalizes homosexuality
description: A new Danish penal law adopted in 1933 removed criminal penalties for consensual homosexual acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Nazi regime bans gay groups and destroys Hirschfeld's institute
description: In 1933 the Nazi party outlawed homosexual organizations and began sending gay men to concentration camps. In May, Nazis burned the library of Magnus Hirschfeld's Institute for Sexual Science in Berlin and wrecked the institution itself.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Philippine law leaves homosexuality outside the criminal code
description: This timeline records the Philippines as decriminalizing homosexuality in 1933, the period when the country's revised penal code — which contains no offense for consensual same-sex acts — took effect.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Soviet decree recriminalizes homosexual acts
description: In 1933 the USSR moved to restore criminal penalties for consensual sex between men, reversing the decriminalization in force since the revolutionary period.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Uruguay decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Uruguay removed consensual same-sex acts between adults from its criminal law in 1934.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Soviet ban on male homosexuality enters the criminal code
description: In 1934 the USSR formally codified the offense of muzhelozhstvo — sex between men — punishable by up to five years in prison, with harsher terms where coercion or minors were involved.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Mona's 440 Club, first US lesbian bar, opens in San Francisco
description: Mona's 440 Club opened in San Francisco in 1936 as the first lesbian bar in the United States. Its waitresses and female performers wore tuxedos, and patrons dressed to match their roles.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Poet Federico García Lorca is executed
description: Spanish poet and playwright Federico García Lorca was shot in 1936, in the opening phase of the Spanish Civil War.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Pink triangle introduced for gay prisoners in Nazi camps
description: From 1937 Nazi concentration camps marked men imprisoned for homosexuality with the pink triangle badge.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The word gay is first spoken on film in a homosexual sense
description: In the 1938 comedy Bringing Up Baby, the word gay was used on screen in reference to homosexuality for the first time.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Diana: A Strange Autobiography breaks ground for lesbian memoir
description: In 1939 Frances V. Rummell, a teacher of French at Stephens College, published Diana: A Strange Autobiography, the first explicitly lesbian autobiography in which two women end up together happily. The publisher added a note vouching that the story was true.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Iceland decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Iceland abolished criminal penalties for consensual homosexual acts in 1940.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Dutch Scientific Humanitarian Committee dissolved amid invasion
description: In May 1940 the NWHK disbanded in the Netherlands as Germany invaded. Members destroyed most of its archive to keep it out of enemy hands, and Nazi soldiers confiscated what remained.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The term transsexuality sees its first recorded use
description: In 1941 the word transsexuality was used for the first time, at that point still in reference to homosexuality and bisexuality.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Switzerland decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Switzerland's national criminal code, in force from 1942, ended prosecution of consensual homosexual acts while setting the age of consent for them at 20.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Cadet scandal erupts in Buenos Aires
description: In 1942 a scandal involving military cadets and homosexuality broke out in Buenos Aires, turning public and official attention onto gay life in Argentina.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sweden decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Sweden lifted its criminal ban on consensual homosexual acts in 1944, fixing the age of consent for them at 18.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Suriname records legalization of homosexuality
description: This timeline lists Suriname as legalizing homosexual acts in 1944; other references date the absence of such an offense earlier, so the year merits checking.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Poet Robert Duncan comes out in print
description: Writing under his own name in the anarchist magazine Politics in 1944, poet Robert Duncan described homosexuals as an oppressed minority — making him the first prominent American to reveal his homosexuality publicly.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Persecution of gay men persists as the Holocaust ends
description: By the end of the Holocaust in 1945, an estimated 3,000 to 9,000 homosexuals had died in Nazi concentration and death camps. An estimated 2,000 to 6,000 gay camp survivors were made to serve out the remainder of their Paragraph 175 sentences in prison.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Queer nightlife returns to Berlin after the war
description: The first gay bar of post-war Berlin opened in the summer of 1945, and that autumn the American sector of West Berlin hosted its first drag ball.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gay veterans form the Veterans Benevolent Association
description: Four honourably discharged gay servicemen created the Veterans Benevolent Association in 1945, the first LGBT veterans' organization in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gay bar Yanagi opens in Japan
description: The bar Yanagi, an early gay venue in Japan, opened its doors in 1945.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: COC, the oldest surviving LGBT organization, forms in the Netherlands
description: The Dutch group COC — its acronym standing for Center for Culture and Recreation — was established in 1946 as one of the earliest homophile organizations and remains the oldest LGBT organization still in existence.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Michael Dillon receives gender-affirming surgery in Britain
description: Plastic surgeon Harold Gillies carried out sex reassignment surgery on Michael Dillon in Britain in 1946.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Lisa Ben creates Vice Versa, the first US lesbian publication
description: Between 1947 and 1948 Edith Eyde, writing as Lisa Ben, typed and self-published Vice Versa in Los Angeles, the first American lesbian publication.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Maricas Unidas Argentinas forms in Argentina
description: The group Maricas Unidas Argentinas came together in 1948 and may have been the first LGBTQ organization in Latin America.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Danish homophile group Forbundet af 1948 is established
description: Forbundet af 1948 — the League of 1948 — took shape in Denmark that year as an organization of homosexuals.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Communist Poland confirms an equal age of consent of 15
description: In 1948 Poland's communist authorities set the age of consent at 15 for all sexual acts, homosexual or heterosexual alike.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Rina Natan becomes Israel's first transgender woman to obtain surgery
description: Rina Natan was the first transgender woman in Israel to undergo sex reassignment surgery of her own volition. The source dates this only to the 1950s; 1956 is the year most often cited.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Swedish federation RFSL is founded
description: The Organization for Sexual Equality — today RFSL, the Swedish Federation for Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual and Transgender Rights — formed in Sweden in 1950.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: East Germany rolls back Nazi expansion of Paragraph 175
description: In 1950 East Germany partially rescinded the Nazi amendments that had broadened Paragraph 175, returning the law against male homosexuality to its narrower pre-1935 form.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Mattachine Society founded in Los Angeles
description: The Mattachine Society was established in Los Angeles on 11 November 1950, becoming the first homosexual rights group in the United States to sustain itself over time.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Lavender Scare purges begin in the US government
description: In 1950, 190 people were dismissed from United States government employment over their sexual orientation, opening the purge era that became known as the Lavender Scare.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)
